mission.title: Install and Configure Kubewarden on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubewarden is a Kubernetes Dynamic Admission Controller that allows you to manage admission policies in your cluster using WebAssembly. Installing Kubewarden enables you to enforce security and compliance policies dynamically as resources are created or modified.

steps.title: Add the Kubewarden Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kubewarden Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kubewarden https://charts.kubewarden.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Kubewarden Controller
steps.description:
Install the Kubewarden Controller using Helm with the following command:
```bash
helm install kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --create-namespace --version v1.32.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kubewarden Controller pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubewarden
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure the Kubewarden Controller has appropriate resource limits, edit the Helm release:
```bash
helm upgrade kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --version v1.32.1
```

steps.title: Set up admission policies
steps.description:
After installation, you can start defining admission policies. Refer to the official documentation for creating policies:
```bash
# Example command to create a policy (customize as needed)
kubectl apply -f your-admission-policy.yaml --namespace kubewarden
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Kubewarden Controller running in the 'kubewarden' namespace, and you will see the pod status as 'Running'. You can then proceed to define and apply your admission policies.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubewarden https://charts.kubewarden.io
helm repo update
helm install kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --create-namespace --version v1.32.1
helm upgrade kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --version v1.32.1

# Example command to create a policy (customize as needed)
kubectl apply -f your-admission-policy.yaml --namespace kubewarden

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kubewarden Controller release using Helm to remove the deployment from your cluster. Run the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Kubewarden. Execute the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd policies.kubewarden.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kubewarden
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Kubewarden namespace and verify that it has been removed. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubewarden
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Kubewarden Controller. You can export the current Helm release values with:
```bash
helm get values kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden > backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kubewarden Controller to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --version v1.33.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, check that the Kubewarden Controller pod is running with the new version. Use this command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubewarden
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubewarden Controller pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors. Use the following command to view logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubewarden
```

troubleshooting.title: Admission policies not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
If admission policies are not being enforced, ensure that the policies are correctly defined and applied. Verify with:
```bash
kubectl get policies --namespace kubewarden
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, confirm that you are using the correct namespace and release name. List all releases with:
```bash
helm list --namespace kubewarden
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubewarden Controller is failing due to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and requests. Adjust them if necessary using:
```bash
helm upgrade kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller --namespace kubewarden --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --version v1.32.1
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed.
